topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A notification about an article, one I had poured months of sleepless nights and agonizing self-doubt into. My heart, already a sluggish weight in my chest, plummeted. The headline was mine, the analysis sharp and deeply personal, yet the byline read: "By Julian Vance." A cold, metallic taste flooded my mouth. I stared at the screen, the pixels blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands started to tremble, and I pressed the heels of them into my eyes, trying to scrub the sight away. How could this happen? It felt like a deliberate erasure, a theft of the very voice I believed was mine. I didn't move for a long time, just sitting there, the silence of my cramped apartment amplifying the frantic, hollow drumming inside my ribs.

[story 2]

I found the proof tucked away in a forgotten corner of my desktop—a draft, a preliminary version of the piece, clearly mine, overlaid with a different author’s signature. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. Every muscle in my body seized up. I walked to the window, the city lights below seeming impossibly bright and indifferent. I felt a profound emptiness settle in my core, a chilling sense that all my efforts amounted to nothing more than borrowed ink. When I finally turned back to the computer, the screen seemed hostile. I didn't scream or cry; instead, a slow, heavy resignation seeped into my posture. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and simply began to clean the dust from my desk, methodical and utterly devoid of energy.

[story 3]

The notification pinged, sharp and unwelcome. I clicked it, and the words swam before my eyes. My carefully constructed narrative, the one I’d labored over with such fierce, fragile hope, was someone else’s achievement. A strange numbness spread through my limbs, a complete shutdown. I felt physically heavy, as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. I stood up, the floor seeming unsteady beneath my feet. I walked to the kitchen, poured myself a glass of water, and watched the liquid ripple. There was no anger, no immediate burst of tears—just a vast, echoing quiet where ambition used to reside. I set the glass down with painstaking slowness, the clink of glass sounding deafening in the sudden vacuum of my attention.
